content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 25, 1976


Mr. George R. Corey
Attorney at Law
790 El Camino Real
Millbrae, California 94030

Dear Mr. Corey:

Thank you for your letter of September 27, 1976, request-
ing an informal opinion concerning advice to be given to your
client, who is a City Councilman. From your above referenced
letter and our telephone discussion of October 7, 1976, the
following facts are presented:

Your client is a City Councilman. From May 15 to June
30, a development company that builds and manages apartment
complexes allowed your client free use of an apartment within
the city. Your client did not live in the apartment, but used
it for a display room in a commercial venture for four weeks.
The fair market value of the apartment for the period used
was over $250. The development company may come before the
various other developments within the city.

On the basis of these facts, you ask the following
question:

If your client now pays the fair market value of the
rent for the six-week period in question, can your client
avoid disclosing use of the apartment as a gift on his
disclosure statement?

Government Code §82028, in pertinent part, defines "gift"
as: "...any payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received". This Section contains no
time provision within which consideration of equal or greater
value must be given, to exclude the payment from being a gift.
Government Code §82044 defines "payment" to mean "...a payment,
distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of
value, whether tangible or intangible". From the above facts,
free use of the apartment property for the specified period,
clearly, would seem to constitute a gift to your client.

George R. Corey
October 25, 1976                                                    Page Two


     Would subsequent delivery of consideration of equal or
greater value by your client to the development company exclude
earlier free use of the apartment from being considered a gift?
The definition of income contained in Government Code §82030,
which includes a gift, may be instructive on this issue. A
gift will not be considered income if the gift is not used
and is returned within 30 days after receipt. Thus not only
must the gift not be used, but it must be returned to the
donor within 30 days in order to be excluded from income, and
hence, reporting. Your client, however, used the gift and
failed, not only to return it to the donor, or to return
consideration of equal or greater value within 30 days, but
appears never to have given consideration of equal or greater
value to the development company in exchange for the gift.
In light of these circumstances, we conclude that:

     1.  free use of the apartment does constitute a gift;

     2.  payment, now, of the fair market value of the
         apartment, would not exclude the free use of
         the apartment from the definition of gift;

     3.  payment, now, of the fair market value of the
         apartment, would not exclude the gift from
         income, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code §82030(b)(4); and

     4.  this gift, as income, would have to be included
         on your client's disclosure statement.

     Another significant issue is raised by the facts in your
case.  Government Code §87100 provides that:

          No public official at any level of state or
          local government shall make, participate in
          making or in any way attempt to use his offi-
          cial position to influence a governmental
          decision in which he knows or has reason to
          know he has a financial interest.

     As a member of the City Council, your client clearly makes,
participates in making and attempts to use his official position
to influence governmental decisions.  Does he have the requisite

George R. Corey
October 25, 1976                                                Page Three


financial interest to trigger the prohibitions of Government
Code §87100? From the facts, your client would even to have
a source of income within the meaning of Government Code
§87103(c). If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision
made or participated in by your client, as a member of the
City Council, would have a material financial effect, dis-
tinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on this
source of income, namely, the development company, your client
would be required to disqualify himself pursuant to the provi-
sions of Government Code §87100.

     I hope that these informal remarks will be of some assis-
tance to you in advising your client as to his duties under the
Political Reform Act. I point out, however, that as you have
not disclosed the identity of the person whose duties are in
question, your client is not immune, pursuant to the provisions
of 2 California Administrative Code §18329(e), from enforce-
ment proceedings based on actions taken by your client in good
faith pursuant to this advice letter. If I may be of further
assistance to you, please let me know.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Edgar A. Kerry
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division


EAK:glb

JOHN J. BIBLE
GEORGE R. COREY
RICHARD W. HENSON
GARY A ORTON
STEVAN N. LUZAICH

PHILIP B. GROTEWOHL
BARRY M. WALLY

F [illegible] COREY, HENSON AND ORTON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
Sep 30 9 29 [illegible] CAMINO REAL
[illegible], CALIFORNIA 94030

September 27, 1976

TELEPHONE
871-3666

Ed Carey, Esq.
Conflict of Interest Division
Fair Campaign Practice Commission
1100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Carey:

I thank you very much for taking the time to discuss a problem
I raised with you by telephone on Friday, September 24th.

I request that your office provide me with an informal opinion
on a fact situation I will set forth below. I am a former
councilman in the City of San Bruno and have as my clients a
number of councilmen from that city and other cities immediately
adjacent to San Bruno. An actual fact situation has arisen that
is described below but I am naturally hesitant to name the
specific client.

The fact situation is:

1. A company who builds and manages apartment complexes
owns a building in Councilman "A"s" city in which there are 60
units.

2. The development company may come before the City
Planning Commission and Council regarding various other developments within the city.

3. The company allowed Councilman "A" to use an apartment
for 6 weeks (May 15 to June 30, 1976) without any charge.

4. The councilman did not live in the apartment but used
it for a display room in a commercial venture. (The actual use
of the apartment was only for 4 weeks.)

5. The apartment unit itself was a "model" and there was
no use for it as the complex was completely filled at the time.

I want to know if I may safely advise the councilman to pay the
fair market value of the rent for the 6 week period in question
with the payment being made at this time, and the use of the

Ed Carey, Esq.
Fair Campaign Practice Commission
September 27, 1976
page two

apartment not being shown as a gift in his disclosure state-
ment. In other words, will the late payment constitute a
rental for value of the apartment.

I will very much appreciate your early attention to this
matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
George R. Corey

GRC:mc

not disclosed the identity of the person whose duties are in 
quest.ion f your client is not inmmne, pursuant to the provisions 
of 2 California Administrative Code §18329(e) , from any enforce-
ment proceedings based on actions taken by your client in good

sections.conclusion: 1. free use of the apartment does constitute a gift; 2. Payment, now, of the fair market value of the apartment, would not exclude the free use of the apartment from the definition of gift; 3. Payment, now, of the fair market value of the apartment, would not exclude the gift from income, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code §82030(b)(4); and 4. this gift, as income, would have to be included on your client's disclosure statement.
sections.question_synthetic: A city councilman received free use of an apartment from a development company for six weeks and used it as a display room. Can he retroactively pay fair market value rent to avoid disclosing this as a gift on his disclosure statement?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that retroactive payment does not exclude the free apartment use from being a gift and must be reported as income on the disclosure statement. Additionally, the councilman may have a disqualifying financial interest in the development company under Government Code §87100 that would require abstention from related Council decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is a standard FPPC advice letter responding to an attorney's request. The document includes both the incoming request letter and FPPC's response. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impact extraction. The FPPC also raised additional concern about potential conflict of interest under §87100.

CONCLUSION: 1. free use of the apartment does constitute a gift; 2. Payment, now, of the fair market value of the apartment, would not exclude the free use of the apartment from the definition of gift; 3. Payment, now, of the fair market value of the apartment, would not exclude the gift from income, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code §82030(b)(4); and 4. this gift, as income, would have to be included on your client's disclosure statement.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that a city councilman's free six-week use of an apartment belonging to a development company constitutes a gift that must be disclosed, and that paying fair market value retroactively does not retroactively convert it into a rental transaction or avoid disclosure requirements.
